title: InstCombine: Stop using nsz in multi-use min/max fold
author: arsenm
patch:
diff --git a/llvm/lib/Transforms/InstCombine/InstCombineSimplifyDemanded.cpp b/llvm/lib/Transforms/InstCombine/InstCombineSimplifyDemanded.cpp
index e175e4213c72a..c570f30585941 100644
--- a/llvm/lib/Transforms/InstCombine/InstCombineSimplifyDemanded.cpp
+++ b/llvm/lib/Transforms/InstCombine/InstCombineSimplifyDemanded.cpp
@@ -3075,9 +3075,10 @@ Value *InstCombinerImpl::SimplifyMultipleUseDemandedFPClass(
       KnownFPClass KnownLHS = computeKnownFPClass(
           CI->getArgOperand(0), DemandedMask, CxtI, Depth + 1);
 
-      return simplifyDemandedFPClassMinMax(
-          Known, IID, CI, DemandedMask, KnownLHS, KnownRHS, F,
-          cast<FPMathOperator>(CI)->hasNoSignedZeros());
+      // Cannot use NSZ in the multiple use case.
+      return simplifyDemandedFPClassMinMax(Known, IID, CI, DemandedMask,
+                                           KnownLHS, KnownRHS, F,
+                                           /*NSZ=*/false);
     }
     default:
       break;
description:
In SimplifyDemandedFPClass, stop using nsz when there's a
mismatch in the sign of 0 for the various min and maxes.

Alive2 doesn't like it: https://alive2.llvm.org/ce/z/ZyhSGA,
presumably because of the possible mismatch between the stored
value and the propagated. Maybe it would be OK if nsz is on all
the uses.

comments.author: arsenm
comments.body:
* **#176579** 👈 (View in Graphite) * `main`

This stack of pull requests is managed by Graphite. Learn more about stacking.

comments.author: dtcxzyw
comments.body:
Does `nsz` allow multiple users observing different signs of zero? In my mind, `nsz` acts as follows:
```
%x = fpop nsz
->
%x = fpop
%is.zero = fcmp oeq %x, 0.0
%zero = select i1 undef, +0.0, -0.0
%res = select i1 %is.zero, %zero, %x
```
It would break a lot of things if we always check one-use for optimizations with nsz.

cc @nunoplopes @regehr 

BTW the counterexample provided by Alive2 looks confusing:
```
Mismatch in pointer(non-local, block_id=0, offset=6)
Source value: poison
Target value: poison
```

comments.author: nikic
comments.body:
I think the issue in this example is the usual problem with non-det operations: Simplifying them requires materializing the non-determinism. You can't make the non-deterministic choice only for one use, you have to make it for all of them.

So here, the minnum nsz can return either +0 or -0, and you choice the -0 result for the function return value. But you do not chose the stored result, so that one could make a conflicting choice of +0. This is not valid, as the values must be the same, not matter how you chose.

comments.author: dtcxzyw
comments.body:
> So here, the minnum nsz can return either +0 or -0, and you choice the -0 result for the function return value. But you do not chose the stored result, so that one could make a conflicting choice of +0. This is not valid, as the values must be the same, not matter how you chose.

This semantic blocks the use of nsz in recursive analysis/simplification. A good thing is that it will address all the issues demonstrated in https://discourse.llvm.org/t/rfc-clarify-the-behavior-of-fp-operations-on-bit-strings-with-nsz-flag/85981.
But I am not sure how significant the performance impact is.

comments.author: nunoplopes
comments.body:
This test reduces to:
```llvm
define half @src(ptr %ptr, half nofpclass(ninf nnorm nsub nan) %arg) {
  %result = call nsz half @llvm.minimum.f32(half %arg, half 0.0)
  store half %result, ptr %ptr
  ret half %result
}

define half @tgt(ptr %ptr, half nofpclass(ninf nnorm nsub nan) %arg) {
  %result = call nsz half @llvm.minimum.f32(half %arg, half 0.0)
  store half %result, ptr %ptr
  ret half 0.0   ;; changed
}
```

The issue is what @nikic describes: non-determinism must be resolved in the same way: %result can evaluate to 0.0 or -0.0, but it is not like undef, it only returns a single value.
If we replace all uses of `%result` with 0.0, the example is fine, but not if only some are replaced like above.

comments.author: jcranmer-intel
comments.body:
`nsz` is, I think, an excellent example of a case where we have a fairly clear operational semantics, but it's not necessarily clear that the operational semantics matches the desired user semantics.

Putting myself in a user's shoes, the number one thing I want from `nsz` is that `a < b ? a : b` be considered equivalent with `fmin(a, b)` (in conjunction with `nnan`, and also insert any other minimum-like function here), and there's a couple of other similar I-don't-care-about-negative-zero transforms that are relevant. We have to stick `nsz` on selects and phis to make this optimization work, so `nsz` already leaves a bit of a bad taste with me in the first place.

At least insofar as currently described, the question is if the nondeterminism of an `nsz` result needs to be resolved at the point of definition or the point of use--if multiple uses can see different values. There are generic arguments for both approaches: different uses seeing different values is definitely confusing for users, but it is also necessary for some optimizations to kick in. But I'm not sure how valuable the missing optimizations are in practice, so I don't know how to make the tradeoff right now.

comments.author: arsenm
comments.body: The only one I think I have queued up is extending the minimumnum/maximumnum case to minnum/maxnum
